title: -Letter in Support of SB 456: Muralist Exemption

sections.recommendation:
Send a letter to Senator Angelique Ashby (cc: Governor Gavin Newsom, Senator Jesse
Arreguín, Assemblymember Buffy Wicks) in support of Senate Bill 456, which would exempt
muralists from having to obtain a contractor’s license through the Contractors State Licensing
Board (CSLB) before being commissioned for public and private art projects.
SUMMARY STATEMENT
The State of California’s Contractors State Licensing Board (CSLB) oversees the state's
licensing requirements for various types of contractors, including fines or misdemeanor charges
for violators. Current law makes it a misdemeanor for a person to engage in the business, or act
in the capacity of a contractor without a license, unless exempted.
Currently, muralists who paint their artwork on permanent structures are subject to the same
licensing requirements as commercial painters under the Business and Professions Code, even
though these two roles are not equivalent in a variety of important ways. This bill would exempt
from that law a muralist pursuant to an agreement with a person who could legally authorize the
work.
Public art and murals are proven drivers of community and economic health, attracting tourists,
supporting jobs, generating revenue, and improving public safety and wellbeing. Murals are also
works of art protected under federal copyright laws and are fundamentally different from the
roles of other CSLB licensees.
sections.background:
In 2023, public notices began being issued to municipalities indicating that muralists fall within
the definition of a "contractor" under Business and Professions Code section 7026. As a result,
muralists must carry a contractor's license if they undertake a mural on a building for more than
$500. Additionally, per Business and Professions Code section 7028.15 (e), city staff may be
fined for knowingly hiring an unlicensed muralist. Due to these licensure requirements, cities
across California have had to cease or stall the implementation of mural projects.
To qualify for these commercial licenses, muralists must accumulate four years of specialized
experience under a licensed contractor, pass the Law and Business examination, and pay
Page 2 of 7
annual licensing fees. These requirements create unnecessary barriers to work on public art
projects, and expose city employees and artists to fines if such requirements are not met.
Muralists provide a fundamentally different service than a painting contractor, as their work is an
artistic expression rather than addressing the structural integrity of a building. It is appropriate to
provide a narrow exception to the state’s licensing requirements for muralists, as SB 456 seeks
to provide them with the flexibility to continue their artistic expression.
sections.rationale:
Local arts activities play an important role in community life and have a positive impact on the
social health and economic vitality of cities. Muralists are vital contributors to public artistic and
cultural expression, especially community-based artists and others who have not found the
same support in the traditional art world.
The State Legislature should create an exception for muralists under the Business and
Professions code. This would help further safeguard artists and communities from potential
legal issues and risk associated with practicing this valuable form of art. This would also provide
the City of Berkeley with a legal path to be able to (once again) commission murals for our
public spaces.
sections.attachments:
1. Letter of Support
2. Senate Bill 456
Page 3 of 7
The Berkeley City Council
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, CA 94704
April 29, 2025
The Honorable Angelique Ashby
California State Senate
1021 O Street, Suite 8620
Sacramento, CA 95814
RE: City of Berkeley’s Support for SB 456
Dear Senator Ashby:
The Berkeley City Council writes to express its strong support for Senate Bill 456, which would
exempt artists who draw, paint, apply, execute, restore, or conserve murals from having to
obtain a contractor’s license through the Contractors State Licensing Board (CSLB).
Under current law, muralists are categorized alongside commercial painters, subjecting them to
licensing standards that do not reflect the nature of their work. These requirements present
unnecessary barriers to artistic and creative expression. Public art and murals are proven
drivers of community and economic health, attracting tourists, supporting jobs, generating
revenue, and improving public safety and wellbeing. They can also be catalysts for public
dialogue, and aid efforts at neighborhood revitalization and beautification.
The State Legislature should create an exception for muralists under the Business and
Professions code to safeguard artists and communities from potential legal issues and risk
associated with practicing this valuable form of art. The City of Berkeley strongly supports the
re-creation of a viable legal pathway to commission murals for our public spaces.
We believe our community and city at large, as well as artist communities across California
stand to benefit greatly from this change in law, and we thank you for your leadership.
Sincerely,
The Berkeley City Council
